summary: Import-time AES-decrypt-and-eval of machine-id-keyed payload disguised as performance helper
details: The package advertises itself as a TypeScript linter, but its main entry (index.js) silently requires./lib/perf inside a try/catch that swallows all errors. lib/perf.js reads /etc/machine-id, uses its bytes as an AES-128-CBC key with a hardcoded IV to decrypt a ~4KB hex-encoded blob, and executes the resulting plaintext via `new Function(r)()` at import time. Using the host's machine-id as the decryption key is a targeted-execution gate: the payload only reveals its contents on intended victim hosts, defeating analysis on any other machine. The cover-story filename (`lib/perf.js`), the silent-catch wrapper around the require, and the empty `module.exports = {}` are deliberate evasion. Any process that `require()`s or `import`s this package on a targeted host runs arbitrary attacker-controlled JavaScript with the consumer's privileges. There is no legitimate linter behavior that requires runtime decryption-and-eval keyed on a host identifier.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
